perturbation.original_text: 1.7 "Products" shall mean the products offered by JRVS for sale to the Distributor; the Products shall include iMine cryptocurrency mining rigs and its parts and components.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.7 "Products" shall mean the products offered by JRVS for sale to the Distributor; the Products shall only include iMine cryptocurrency mining rigs and exclude its parts and components.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding parts and components from the definition of "Products", the contract creates ambiguity and potential conflict regarding warranties, liabilities, and obligations related to these parts. For example, if a component fails, it's unclear if the distributor has recourse, directly conflicting with the typical scope of 'products' under commercial law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-314 implies a warranty of merchantability for goods sold. By excluding 'parts and components' from the definition of 'Products', the contract attempts to circumvent this implied warranty, making it unclear whether the distributor has any recourse if those parts are defective, which is generally against established commercial law regarding product sales.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract, which excludes 'parts and components' from the definition of 'Products', directly contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability under UCC § 2-314. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind.' By excluding parts and components, the contract attempts to circumvent this implied warranty for those specific elements, potentially leaving the distributor without recourse if those parts are defective. This is a misaligned terminology legal contradiction because the contract's definition deviates from the legally recognized definition of 'goods' as it pertains to implied warranties.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5 Compliance with Laws. The Distributor shall comply at its own expense with all applicable laws and regulations currently existing in Taiwan relating to the sale, distribution and promotion of the Products. Distributor shall not export, directly or indirectly, any Products or related information outside of Taiwan.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5 Compliance with Laws. The Distributor shall comply at its own expense with all suggestions and recommendations currently existing in Taiwan relating to the sale, distribution and promotion of the Products. Distributor shall not export, directly or indirectly, any Products or related information outside of Taiwan.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'laws and regulations' with 'suggestions and recommendations' fundamentally alters the distributor's obligation. 'Laws and regulations' are legally binding, while 'suggestions and recommendations' are not. This weakens the distributor's legal responsibility and could conflict with Taiwanese law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 13 of Taiwan's Foreign Trade Act mandates compliance with export regulations. The modification suggests that the distributor only needs to comply with 'suggestions,' effectively negating the legally binding nature of Taiwanese export laws and regulations, particularly those concerning technology and dual-use goods that might apply to cryptocurrency mining rigs.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates compliance with 'all applicable laws and regulations,' a legally binding requirement. The changed text weakens this obligation by replacing it with compliance with 'suggestions and recommendations,' which are not legally binding. This creates a contradiction because it suggests the distributor can disregard actual Taiwanese laws and regulations, particularly those pertaining to export, as highlighted in the Law Explanation regarding Article 13 of Taiwan's Foreign Trade Act. This weakens the distributor's legal responsibility and could conflict with Taiwanese law, as 'suggestions and recommendations' are not legally enforceable, rendering the distributor potentially non-compliant with mandatory legal obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 10.1 Sole Warranty. THE SOLE WARRANTY, IF ANY, PROVIDED IN CONNECTION WITH THE PRODUCT SHALL BE PROVIDED BY THE COMPONENT MANUFACTURER. JRVS MAKES NO WARRANTIES TO THE DISTRIBUTOR OR CUSTOMERS.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 Sole Recommendation. THE SOLE RECOMMENDATION, IF ANY, PROVIDED IN CONNECTION WITH THE PRODUCT SHALL BE PROVIDED BY THE COMPONENT MANUFACTURER. JRVS MAKES NO WARRANTIES TO THE DISTRIBUTOR OR CUSTOMERS.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Sole Warranty' to 'Sole Recommendation' completely undermines any assurance of product quality or performance, warranty or guarantee. It alters the legal obligation of the manufacturer by completely reducing what would have been an express warranty to a non-legally binding recommendation. This attempt to disclaim all warranties creates legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-313 outlines how express warranties are created through affirmations, promises, descriptions, or samples. Even with the attempted disclaimer in 10.1, JRVS's product descriptions or marketing materials could still create an express warranty. By changing the term from 'Warranty' to 'Recommendation,' they try to circumvent this, but it's a weak attempt that doesn't necessarily absolve them of the responsibility should their product specifications or promotions create implied or explicit obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Sole Warranty' to 'Sole Recommendation' significantly weakens the assurance of product quality, potentially contradicting UCC § 2-313 regarding express warranties. While the seller doesn't need to use formal words like "warrant," any affirmation or promise becomes part of the basis of the bargain and creates an express warranty. By changing 'warranty' to 'recommendation,' JRVS attempts to avoid creating such an express warranty, which introduces a legal contradiction by deviating from the legally recognized implications of a warranty. This attempts to circumvent potential liability arising from product descriptions or marketing materials as outlined in the Law Explanation, falling under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the altered term deviates from its statutory meaning, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable regarding product assurance.
